# Checksum Best Practices

*Note: as you are reading this document and our official documentation, take note of anything not clear or obvious. Part of your role will be to keep the internal and external docs up-to-date (this is a team effort\!)*

* Shout out your contribution in the checksum-customer-eng channel on slack\!

---

# Locators

await page.locator()

* Create **state-independent locators** that work for all users and environments.  
  * Do not hard code a specific element name to grab a page element. Account data will change over time and locators need to be dynamic enough to account for things like data-wipes.  
* Prefer HTML **attributes** that rarely change, such as:   
  * data-testid  
  * role  
  * placeholder  
  * label  
* Static text is fine when appropriate.  
  * .getByRole(“button”, { name: “Submit” })  
* Where possible, use **regular expressions** instead of hard-coded text.  
  * .getByText(“Download was successful\!”) is better written as .getByText(/(download|success)/i)  
* For dates, use values relative to today and account for weekdays, weekends, and month boundaries.  
* **Avoid .nth() selectors\!** Unless the index is part of the logic. Selecting the .first() item in a list is acceptable, but never use nth just to choose an element among duplicate buttons.  
* When writing a description for an action or assertion, we must include both a **What** and a **Why**  
  * What is the action trying to achieve  
  * Why is it trying to achieve it

  ![][image1]

* Variables should also be wrapped using a ChecksumAI wrapper so that the AI is given context to the variable in question (see image above)

---

# Race Conditions

We work on development and staging environments set up for testing before features reach production. The infra isn’t as good as production. Stability will be at odds to how fast the Playwright bot can navigate a customer's app\! Always consider the state-of-the-app between actions (Playwright is lightning fast\!).

* The most common Playwright actions and assertions wait for elements to load automatically. This is referred to as a web-first assertion. Prefer these locators. See a list of web-first assertions here: [https://playwright.dev/docs/test-assertions](https://playwright.dev/docs/test-assertions)   
  * If a button exists on the target page then a simple navigate-then-click is enough. (No need to first assert the page has loaded before performing your click action)  
  * If the same button exists on the previous page then assert a different element on the target page to affirm the target page has loaded. (to avoid false-positives during the test run)  
* When extracting text from the page the element can render before data arrives. Use **expect.toHaveText** using regex to confirm the content is populated correctly, before extracting and saving within a variable.  
* If a web-first assertion is impossible (e.g. verifying list sorting), use **expect.poll**  
* **waitForResponse** can confirm an element is ready, but use it sparingly.  
* Avoid using **waitForTimeout** whenever possible\!  
  * This is very important since it sticks out to our customers and is commonly represented as poor practice.  
* For elements that appear only sometimes (such as onboarding shown every 30 days), use **addLocatorHander()** or a **try/catch statement** with a **short timeout** within the first action/assertion in the try ( **e.g. .click({ timeout: 5\_000 })** ) \- instead of an if statement to prevent race conditions.

---

# State Isolation

* Tests need to be designed to work on different environments and users since many of our customers use environments interchangeably (today run on staging, the next run the same tests on production).  
  * Avoid relying on the existing app’s state. Write tests in a way that makes them data independent.  
  * You can assume some basic account configuration will exist as a part of the overall account setup. For example, in an e-commerce store you can assume the storefront itself already exists and is connected to Payment processing.  
* Data build-up and tear-down is common for our customers.  
  * **Use API calls** instead of relying on user-interface actions  
    * For example, make a **GET** request for a list of current table elements, then make a **DELETE** request for each element found  
    * **Don’t** **create a loop** that grabs the elements and for each element, click the dropdown, click delete, click confirm, wait for page to refresh, repeat.  
* As a rule-of-thumb, handle state that is directly related to the test and changes on a day-to-day basis. Apply here the 20/80 rule. Lock the 20% of the state that accounts for 80% of the state that may change.  
* There will be times when you must rely on state because creating it via API requires heavy integration or many API calls in continuation. In these cases, try to have “**DO NOT DELETE**” in the resource name and ensure it is excluded from any clean-up API. This naming convention serves as a flag to the team that test cases may rely upon it and should be handled with extreme care.

---

# Test Architecture

* Wrap every web action (click, fill, etc) with **checksumAI**. Use a clear and comprehensive description.  
  * await checksumAI("Click the close button above the store section to close the payment window", async () \=\> ( \<action here\> )  
* Provide a message string to every assertion.  
  * await expect( \<element\>, "Verify the warning dialog is visible before proceeding" ).toBeVisible();  
* **Write** **detailed descriptions** for all actions/assertions in a way that enables any engineer on the team to understand the test without having prior knowledge or context\!  
  * Be empathetic towards this goal since one-day it will be you on the receiving end.  
* Use **test.step()** to group large flows together to improve test readability.  
  * Test.step to wrap the actions used for creating a product, then test.step to wrap the actions used for placing an order, etc.  
* Give every function, constant, variable, and page object a descriptive name; add docstrings explaining purpose and arguments.  
  * Don’t use product1, product2, product3  
* House each test within its own file. Call defineChecksumTest once per file with a unique ID  
  * defineChecksumTest( "Verify that the File Library Supports all file Formats", "oLwDh" )

---

# Functions

* Do not use **vs: IVariableStore** as an argument within functions, and do not use variableStore variables within functions\!  
  * All variables used within a function should be **included as arguments**, with the exception of variables used within the scope of the function itself that are temporary

---

# Variable Store and Constants

* Use the variable store fixture **vs.** for shared test values and store short-lived values in **const** variables  
  * vs.taskName \= \`cktest-${Date,now()}\`

---

# Environments, Users, and Auth

* The **playwright.config.ts** and **checksum.config.ts** are the heart and life of every testing suite.  
  * Become very familiar with these files. Anything you do not know today should be remedied as early as possible\! Do not expect to learn the utility of these files from simply writing/fixing tests.  
* Define each environment's baseURL, username/password in checksum.config.ts via environment variables.  
  * Assert required environment variables at the top of the config so tests fail fast if any are missing  
  * if ( \!process.env.CHECKSUM\_API\_KEY ) { throw new Error("Missing environment variables in checksum.config.ts"); }  
* In tests, call **getEnvironment** (returned by the init() function) to access environment details.  
  * const { test, defineChecksumTest, expect, checksumAI, **getEnvironment** } \= init();  
    const { user, environment } \= getEnvironment({ name: "newtonresearch-develop", userRole: "default", });  
    

---

# Toast Messages

* Regarding popups (modals)  
  * If they always appear \- it’s better to add them to the test flow \- assert and close or wait until hidden.  
  * If not all the time \- add locator handler for them: [https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-page\#page-add-locator-handler](https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-page#page-add-locator-handler)

---

# Misc.

* When creating app data name it using our **standard convention**: cktest-${Date.now()}  
  * vs.productName \= \`cktest-product-${Date.now()}\`  
  * cktest can always be deleted with a cleanup utility  
    * Only elements named with the DO NOT DELETE should be avoided by cleanup  
* Import the **login()** helper directly; do not invoke it through init()  
* **Never** hard-code URLs\!  
* Do not import page or expect from playwright  
  * import { Page, expect } from "@playwright/test";  
* Always import page or expect from checksum  
  * import { IChecksumPage, IChecksumExpect } from "@checksum-ai/runtime";  
* Page objects are Checksum-specific. Obtain one with .getPage() method, which returns an IChecksumPage  
* **Files** used during tests for **uploading**/reading/writing must be stored within the **checksum/test-data/bin** directory.  
  * Retrieve files using our built in method:  
    * page.resolveAssetsFolder(\["\<relative path from bin directory\>"\])  
* **Never** **use** await page.waitForLoadState(“networkidle”)  
* To interact with Canvas elements use coordinates  
* **Never use** ‘as string’ or ‘ || “”’ to avoid Typescript complaints because failures become increasingly difficult to diagnose  
  * e.g. baseURL: process.env.STAGING\_BASE\_URL as string  
  * e.g. const user \= await page.locator(‘h1’).textContent() || “”

[image1]: <data:image/png;base64,>
